text: The U.S. Treasury 2-year par yield edged down 1 basis point to 4.19% on June 18, 2026, from 4.20% on June 17, essentially holding the level it had jumped to after the prior day's hawkish FOMC as the market kept pricing a 2026 rate hike.

The U.S. Treasury 10-year par yield fell 3 basis points to 4.46% on June 18, 2026, from 4.49% on June 17, partially unwinding the prior session's hawkish-FOMC spike as easing oil prices trimmed the inflation risk premium in the long end.

The U.S. Treasury 30-year par yield slipped 3 basis points to 4.90% on June 18, 2026, from 4.93% on June 17, with the 5-year down 4 basis points to 4.23%, as the belly led a modest, broad-based easing across the curve.

The 2s10s Treasury par-yield spread bull-flattened 2 basis points to +27 basis points on June 18, 2026, calculated as the 10-year par yield of 4.46% minus the 2-year par yield of 4.19%, down from +29 basis points on June 17, as the long end eased while the policy-anchored front end held its post-FOMC level.

subject.name: Bank of England Monetary Policy Committee

text: The Bank of England held Bank Rate at 3.75% on June 18, 2026, in a 7-2 vote, with two of the nine MPC members - reported to be Huw Pill and Megan Greene, who had signalled hawkish intent - dissenting in favour of an immediate 25 basis point hike, as UK CPI held at 2.8% in May and the unemployment rate ticked down to 4.9%.

The Swiss National Bank held its policy rate at 0.00% on June 18, 2026, and nudged its inflation forecast up to 0.6% for both 2026 and 2027 (from 0.5%) while reiterating that its readiness to intervene in foreign-exchange markets has increased, citing Middle East fragility as a key uncertainty.

The U.S. Dollar Index rose to 100.72 on June 18, 2026, its highest level since May 2025, extending the prior day's advance from 100.47 as the hawkish Fed shift and the dollar's widening yield advantage kept the euro and yen on the back foot even as U.S. yields eased.

WTI crude slipped to around $75 a barrel on June 18, 2026, dipping below $75 to its lowest since early March, as the U.S.-Iran interim agreement and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz to tanker traffic drained the war risk premium; Brent traded near $79, down about 1.6% on the day.

U.S. initial jobless claims fell 4,000 to a seasonally adjusted 226,000 for the week ended June 13, 2026, roughly in line with the 225,000 Reuters consensus, while continuing claims rose 24,000 to 1.81 million for the week ended June 6 and the median duration of unemployment reached 11.6 weeks in May, the longest since November 2021 - a labor market that is stable but slowly cooling.

subject.name: US-Iran interim agreement and Strait of Hormuz reopening

text: The U.S.-Iran interim agreement and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, with maritime authorities guiding tankers back through the strait, was the dominant macro driver on June 18, 2026: it pulled crude to its lowest since early March and removed the energy-price tail risk that the FOMC had explicitly cited the day before as a reason for its hawkish projections, even as the BoE and SNB stayed on hold.
